text: The reasons for the dissociation of carbon−halogen bonds in copper catalysed cross coupling reactions remain unclear due to the multiplicity of potential redox states available to the transition metal that catalyses the reactions and due to the predicted instability of the potentially involved high oxidation state metal−carbene or −carboid species. Herein we show that α-haloacetonitriles react with copper(I) ions and neutral copper(I) complexes through oxidative addition affording the otherwise unknown copper(III) complexes and enabling structural analysis thereof. The stability of these elusive copper(III) species is accounted for by a robust Cu−CF₃ bond and a high kinetic barrier for reductive elimination of C(CF₃)−C(CH₂CN). The mechanism for the oxidative addition of α-haloacetonitriles to ionic copper(I) complexes proceeds in a manner closely analogous to an SN2 mechanism; however neutral copper(I) complexes react through a simple halogen atom transfer. There is a strong ligand acceleration of the oxidative addition reaction that correlates quantitatively to the rates of copper catalysed cross coupling of alkyl electrophiles with azoles, amines and alkynes.
explanation: The text shows technical, well-formed chemistry content and plausible mechanistic details that resemble scientific writing; its coherence and specificity could indicate a human-authored abstract, though polished AI-generated text could also resemble this style.
